question: What is a star?
answer: A huge ball of hot gas that gives out light, like the Sun
accept: a giant ball of hot gas that emits light
misconceptions: a star is a planet that twinkles
a star is a lump of rock
the Sun is not a star

question: What is a planet?
answer: A spherical object that orbits a star
accept: a round/ball-shaped object that goes around a star
misconceptions: a planet is flat or a circle

question: State the difference between what a moon orbits and what a planet orbits.
answer: A moon orbits a planet
a planet orbits a star
accept: moons go around planets; planets go around stars/the Sun

question: Name the eight planets in our solar system in order from the Sun.
answer: Mercury, Venus, Earth, Mars
Jupiter, Saturn
Uranus, Neptune
accept: all eight named in correct order
misconceptions: listing the Moon as a planet
listing the Sun as a planet

question: What is a galaxy?
answer: A huge collection of billions of stars
held together by gravity
accept: a massive group of stars bound by gravity
misconceptions: a galaxy is the same as a solar system
a galaxy is a single star
a galaxy is a planet and its moons

question: What is the name of our galaxy?
answer: The Milky Way
accept: Milky Way

question: What shape of galaxy is the Milky Way?
answer: A spiral galaxy
accept: spiral
misconceptions: a flat disc with no shape

question: What is an orbit?
answer: The path one object takes around another in space
accept: the route an object follows around another object
misconceptions: an orbit is a perfect circle
an orbit is the object itself
an orbit only means going around the Sun

question: What causes the planets to orbit the Sun?
answer: The gravitational pull of the Sun
the object in the centre pulls the orbiting object
accept: the Sun's gravity
gravitational pull of the central object
misconceptions: there is no force, they just float

question: The paths of the planets look like circles. What shape are orbits actually, and what does this word mean?
answer: Elliptical
slightly squashed circles
accept: ellipses; squashed/stretched circles

question: Name the four rocky planets in our solar system.
answer: Mercury, Venus
Earth, Mars
accept: all four rocky planets named

question: Pluto orbits the Sun but is not classed as a planet. What is it classed as, and give one reason why.
answer: A dwarf planet
it does not dominate its orbit / is not significantly larger than other objects on a similar orbit
accept: dwarf planet; it does not clear/dominate its orbit
misconceptions: Pluto is too cold to be a planet

question: Explain the difference between a solar system and a galaxy.
answer: A solar system is a star with the objects that orbit it
a galaxy is a huge collection of billions of stars (and is far bigger)
accept: a galaxy contains billions of stars whereas a solar system is just one star and its objects
misconceptions: a solar system is bigger than a galaxy
a galaxy is just our Sun and its planets

question: Why do astronomers use light years instead of metres to measure distances in space?
answer: Because distances in space are so huge that the numbers in metres would be unmanageably large
accept: space is so big that metres give numbers that are far too big
misconceptions: because light years measure time more easily
because metres do not work in space
because light years are smaller than metres

question: A student says 'a light year is a really long amount of time.' Explain what is wrong with this statement.
answer: A light year is a distance, not a time
it is how far light travels in one year
accept: it measures distance not time
misconceptions: the student is correct, it is a long time
it measures the speed of light
it is the time light has existed

question: Where do stars form, and what are these regions called?
answer: Stars form when dust and gas come together due to gravity
in clouds of dust and gas called nebulae
accept: in nebulae; from gas and dust pulled together by gravity
misconceptions: stars form in galaxies' centres only

question: Each of the planets takes a different amount of time to orbit the Sun. Give two reasons why.
answer: They travel different lengths of path around the Sun (closer or further from the Sun)
they travel at different speeds
accept: different orbit distances; different speeds
misconceptions: they all take the same time
the Sun moves them at different times

question: Which of these correctly describes our Sun?
answer: A star
distractors: the Sun is not a planet, planets orbit it
a galaxy is a collection of billions of stars, not a single star
a moon is a lump of rock that orbits a planet

question: Which list contains only gas planets?
answer: Jupiter, Saturn, Uranus, Neptune
distractors: these are the four rocky planets, not gas planets
Earth and Mars are rocky planets, not gas planets
Mercury is a rocky planet, not a gas planet

question: What does a moon orbit?
answer: A planet
distractors: planets orbit a star, but moons orbit planets
a galaxy is a collection of stars, not something a moon orbits
moons orbit planets, not other moons

question: Which statement best describes a light year?
answer: The distance light travels in one year
options: The time it takes light to leave a star
The distance light travels in one year
The age of a star in Earth years
The speed at which light travels
distractors: a light year is a distance, not a time
a light year measures distance, not age
a light year is a distance, not a speed
misconceptions: a light year is a unit of time
a light year is a speed

question: What shape are planets?
answer: Spherical
distractors: a circle is 2D, but planets are three-dimensional balls
planets are not cubes, they are round
planets are round spheres, not star-shaped

question: What keeps a planet orbiting the Sun rather than flying off into space?
answer: The gravitational pull of the Sun
options: The gravitational pull of the Sun
distractors: heat does not hold a planet in orbit, gravity does
planets do not give out their own light to stay in orbit
orbits are caused by the central Sun's gravity, not distant galaxies
misconceptions: heat from the Sun holds planets in orbit
planets stay in orbit on their own

question: Which object is the largest?
answer: A galaxy
distractors: a planet is far smaller than a whole solar system or galaxy
a solar system is one star and its objects, much smaller than a galaxy of billions of stars
a moon is one of the smallest objects listed
misconceptions: a solar system is bigger than a galaxy

question: Sound travels at 330 m/s in air. Using distance = speed x time, calculate how far sound travels in 2 seconds.
working: distance = speed x time = 330 x 2 = 660 m
accept: 660 metres

question: Sound travels at 330 m/s in air. Using distance = speed x time, calculate how far sound travels in 1 minute.
working: t = 1 min = 60 s; distance = speed x time = 330 x 60 = 19 800 m
accept: 19800 metres
misconceptions: 330 m (forgot to convert minutes to seconds)

question: Sound travels at 1000 m/s in a solid. Using distance = speed x time, calculate how far it travels in 2 minutes.
working: t = 2 min = 120 s; distance = speed x time = 1000 x 120 = 120 000 m
accept: 120000 metres
misconceptions: 2000 m (forgot to convert minutes to seconds)

question: Light travels at 300 000 000 m/s. Using distance = speed x time, calculate how far light travels in 1 second.
working: distance = speed x time = 300 000 000 x 1 = 300 000 000 m
accept: 300 million metres

question: Light travels at 300 000 000 m/s. Using distance = speed x time, calculate how far light travels in 1 minute.
working: t = 1 min = 60 s; distance = speed x time = 300 000 000 x 60 = 18 000 000 000 m
accept: 18 billion metres

question: How many of the eight planets in our solar system have moons?
working: There are 224 moons spread among six of the eight planets (Mercury and Venus have none).
accept: six
misconceptions: 224 (confused number of moons with number of planets)

question: What is the total number of moons in our solar system?
working: In total there are 224 moons in our solar system, spread among six of the eight planets.

question: How many moons does Jupiter have, according to the handbook?
working: Jupiter, for instance, has 92 moons.
misconceptions: 4 (only the moons shown in the photo)

question: Approximately how far from Earth is Alpha Centauri, our next closest star, in light years?
working: Alpha Centauri is about 4.3 light years away from Earth.
answer: 4.3 light years
accept: 4.3 ly
misconceptions: 8 light years (confused with light from the Sun taking 8 minutes)
0.000015 light years (that is the Sun's distance)

question: Light from the Sun takes about how many minutes to reach Earth?
working: The Sun is 0.000015 light years away, which means light takes about 8 minutes to reach us.
answer: 8 minutes
accept: about 8 minutes
8 min
misconceptions: 1 year (a light year is the distance, not the travel time to the Sun)

question: How many moons does Earth have?
working: Earth is the only planet in our solar system to have just one moon.
accept: one

question: What causes day and night on Earth?
answer: The Earth spins on its axis once every 24 hours
options: The Earth orbits the Sun once a year
The Earth spins on its axis once every 24 hours
The Sun moves around the Earth
The Earth's tilt changes through the day
distractors: the orbit takes a whole year and is not what causes day and night
the Sun does not move around the Earth; the Earth spins
the tilt stays fixed and does not change through the day
misconceptions: we get day and night because the Earth orbits the Sun
the Sun travels around the Earth
day and night are caused by the Earth's tilt changing

question: By how many degrees is the Earth's axis tilted from vertical?
distractors: the axis is not vertical; it is tilted
45° is too large; the tilt is 23.4°
90° would mean the axis lies in the orbital plane, which is wrong
misconceptions: the Earth's axis is straight up and down (not tilted)

question: Why is the northern hemisphere warmer in summer?
answer: It is tilted towards the Sun, so it receives more direct sunlight
options: The Earth is closer to the Sun in summer
It is tilted towards the Sun, so it receives more direct sunlight
The Sun gives out more heat in summer
The Earth's tilt becomes larger in summer
distractors: the small change in distance from the tilt cannot account for the temperature change
the Sun's output does not change with our seasons
the tilt does not become larger; it stays fixed at 23.4°
misconceptions: the seasons are caused by being closer to or further from the Sun
the Sun gives out more energy in summer
the tilt of the Earth changes through the year

question: When it is summer in the UK, what season is it in Australia?
answer: Winter
options: The same season as the UK
distractors: the opposite hemisphere experiences the opposite season, not the same
Australia is in the southern hemisphere, so it is winter, not spring
the seasons are opposite, not the same, between hemispheres
misconceptions: both hemispheres have the same season at the same time
students think of Australia as on the front/back of the world rather than the opposite hemisphere

question: Which type of sunlight makes a surface warmer?
answer: Direct light, because the same energy is concentrated on a smaller area
options: Glancing light, because it covers a bigger area
Direct light, because the same energy is concentrated on a smaller area
Glancing light, because it travels further
distractors: spreading the same light over a bigger area makes it dimmer and cooler, not warmer
they do not warm equally; direct light is more intense
the distance the light travels is not the reason; it is how spread out it is
misconceptions: glancing and direct light warm the surface equally
glancing light is warmer because it spreads out more

question: What does the Earth spin around to give us day and night?
answer: Its axis
distractors: the equator is a line around the middle, not what the Earth spins around
the Earth orbits the Sun once a year; it spins on its axis for day and night
the Earth does not spin around the Moon
misconceptions: the Earth spins around the Sun to give day and night
the Earth spins around the equator

question: Why do countries on the equator not have very different summers and winters?
answer: They experience the Sun at roughly the same angle all year round
options: They are always tilted towards the Sun
They experience the Sun at roughly the same angle all year round
The Sun is always directly overhead at midnight
They are closer to the Sun all year
distractors: the equator is not always tilted towards the Sun; it is roughly side-on all year
the Sun is not overhead at midnight; the angle stays similar through the year
distance from the Sun does not cause the seasons
misconceptions: the equator is always tilted towards the Sun
equatorial regions are closer to the Sun

question: How many hours does it take the Earth to complete one full spin on its axis?
answer: 24 hours
working: One full spin of the Earth on its axis takes 24 hours, which gives us one day.
misconceptions: a full spin takes a year

question: How many days does it take the Earth to orbit the Sun once (one year)?
answer: 365.25 days
working: One orbit of the Earth around the Sun is one year, which takes 365.25 days.

question: It is midday for a flag facing the Sun. How many hours later will that flag have spun halfway round to face away from the Sun (midnight)?
answer: 12 hours
working: One full spin takes 24 hours, so half a spin (from facing the Sun to facing away) takes 24 ÷ 2 = 12 hours.
misconceptions: it takes 24 hours to go from day to night

question: A diagram splits each hemisphere into 10 equal 'chunks'. In summer, 6 of the northern hemisphere's chunks are lit by the Sun. What fraction of the hemisphere is lit?
working: Lit chunks ÷ total chunks = 6 ÷ 10 = 0.6 of the hemisphere is lit.
misconceptions: both hemispheres are always lit equally
the whole hemisphere is always lit

question: Using the 10-chunk model, the northern hemisphere has 6 chunks lit in summer. How many chunks of the southern hemisphere are lit at the same time?
answer: 4 chunks
working: Each hemisphere has 10 chunks. If the northern hemisphere has 6 lit, the southern has 10 - 6 = 4 lit, because it is tilted away from the Sun.
misconceptions: both hemispheres have the same number of chunks lit
the southern hemisphere has 6 lit too

question: In the 10-chunk model, the lit chunks represent daylight. If the northern hemisphere has 6 of its 10 chunks lit in summer, this is the same fraction as how many hours of daylight in a 24-hour day?
answer: 14.4 hours
working: Fraction lit = 6 ÷ 10 = 0.6; daylight hours = 0.6 × 24 = 14.4 hours.
misconceptions: daylight is always 12 hours regardless of season

question: In winter the southern-hemisphere model shows 4 of its 10 chunks lit. In a 24-hour day, how many hours of daylight does this represent?
answer: 9.6 hours
working: Fraction lit = 4 ÷ 10 = 0.4; daylight hours = 0.4 × 24 = 9.6 hours.
misconceptions: winter and summer have the same daylight hours
the days get longer in winter

question: If summer gives 14.4 hours of daylight and winter gives 9.6 hours of daylight, what is the difference in daylight hours between the two seasons?
answer: 4.8 hours
working: Difference = summer daylight - winter daylight = 14.4 - 9.6 = 4.8 hours.
misconceptions: there is no difference in daylight between seasons

question: If daylight in the northern hemisphere is 16 hours on a summer's day, how many hours of darkness (night) are there in that 24-hour day?
answer: 8 hours
working: Night = 24 - daylight = 24 - 16 = 8 hours. In summer the days are long and the nights are short.
misconceptions: day and night are always 12 hours each
in summer the nights are long

question: For a country on the equator the Sun hits at the same angle all year, so daylight stays at 12 hours. By how many hours does its daylight change between summer and winter?
answer: 0 hours
working: Equatorial daylight does not alter, so the change = 12 - 12 = 0 hours, because it is never tilted towards or away from the Sun.
misconceptions: the equator has big seasonal changes in daylight
the equator is always tilted towards the Sun

question: A torch shines the same beam onto paper. Held directly it lights a 2 cm length; tilted to a glancing angle it lights 2.5 cm. By how many cm has the lit length increased?
answer: 0.5 cm
working: Increase = glancing length - direct length = 2.5 - 2.0 = 0.5 cm. The same light spread over a longer length is dimmer.
misconceptions: direct light covers more area than glancing light
tilting the surface does not change the lit area

question: What is the Earth's axis?
answer: The (tilted) line through the poles / joining the North Pole to the South Pole that the Earth spins around
accept: the line from the North Pole to the South Pole
the tilted line the Earth spins around
misconceptions: the axis is the Earth's orbit around the Sun

question: Explain why one side of the Earth experiences daytime while the other has night.
answer: Light from the Sun travels in straight lines
so the side facing the Sun is lit up (daytime)
and the side facing away is in shadow/dark (night)
accept: the Sun can only light the side facing it
light cannot bend around to the far side
misconceptions: the whole Earth is lit at once
night happens because the Sun switches off

question: A student says we get day and night because the Earth orbits the Sun. Explain what is wrong with this.
answer: Day and night are caused by the Earth spinning on its axis once every 24 hours, not by the orbit (which takes a whole year)
accept: the orbit takes a year, so it cannot cause day and night
it is the spin, not the orbit, that gives day and night
misconceptions: day and night are caused by the orbit

question: A student says the seasons happen because the Earth's tilt changes during the year. Explain why this is wrong.
answer: The tilt of the Earth stays fixed (does not change)
the seasons happen because a hemisphere tilts towards or away from the Sun as the Earth orbits
accept: the tilt is always 23.4° and does not change
the direction of tilt stays the same as Earth orbits
misconceptions: the tilt changes through the year
the amount of tilt grows in summer

question: Many students think the seasons are caused by the Earth being closer to or further from the Sun. Why is this explanation wrong?
answer: The small change in distance caused by the tilt is far too tiny to account for the temperature changes over the year
accept: the difference in distance is too small to matter
it is the angle of the sunlight, not the distance, that matters
misconceptions: summer happens when the Earth is closest to the Sun
distance from the Sun controls the seasons

question: Describe the two motions of the Earth and what each one causes.
answer: The Earth spins on its axis (once every 24 hours), which causes day and night
the Earth orbits the Sun (once a year), which is linked to the seasons
accept: spinning gives day/night, orbiting gives the year
rotation = day and night; orbit = year/seasons
misconceptions: the spin and the orbit are the same motion

question: Explain fully why it is summer in the northern hemisphere when the North Pole is tilted towards the Sun.
answer: When the North Pole is tilted towards the Sun the sunlight hits the northern hemisphere directly / head-on
direct light is more concentrated/intense, so it has more of a warming effect
this makes it summer in the northern hemisphere
accept: the light hits more directly so warms more
more direct sunlight means more warming
misconceptions: it is summer because the Earth is closer to the Sun
the Sun is hotter in summer

question: Compare what happens to the South Pole when the North Pole is tilted towards the Sun.
answer: When the North Pole is tilted towards the Sun, the South Pole is tilted away from the Sun
so the northern hemisphere has summer while the southern hemisphere has winter
accept: the South Pole points away from the Sun
north tilts towards, south tilts away
misconceptions: both poles tilt towards the Sun at once
both hemispheres are in summer together

question: Using the idea of spreading butter on bread, explain why glancing sunlight feels less warm than direct sunlight.
answer: Glancing light spreads the same amount of light over a bigger area
so it is more thinly spread (like butter on a big slice) and gives less warmth/is dimmer
accept: the energy is spread out more so each bit is weaker
spread over a larger area means less warming per area
misconceptions: glancing light contains less energy in total
direct and glancing light warm equally

question: Name the imaginary line around the middle of the Earth that separates the two hemispheres.
answer: The equator
accept: equator

question: What do we call the half of the Earth above the equator?
answer: The northern hemisphere
accept: northern hemisphere
misconceptions: the top half is the southern hemisphere
it is called the North Pole

question: Explain why the northern hemisphere has longer days than nights during summer.
answer: In summer the northern hemisphere is tilted towards the Sun, so more of it is lit by the Sun at once
so it spends more of each 24-hour spin in daylight, giving long days and short nights
accept: more of the hemisphere is in sunlight, so daylight lasts longer
being tilted towards the Sun means more time in the light
misconceptions: day and night are always equal

question: When the northern hemisphere has its most hours of daylight, what is happening to the southern hemisphere's hours of daylight?
answer: The southern hemisphere has its fewest hours of daylight (shortest days)
accept: southern days are at their shortest
the southern hemisphere has long nights
misconceptions: both hemispheres have long days at the same time
the southern hemisphere also has its longest days

question: It is autumn in the northern hemisphere. What season is it in the southern hemisphere?
answer: Spring
accept: it is spring in the south
misconceptions: it is autumn in both hemispheres
it is winter in the south

question: Describe what happens to the temperature and the length of the days in the UK as it moves from summer into autumn.
answer: The day length decreases (days get shorter)
and the weather/temperature gets colder
accept: shorter days and cooler weather
days shorten and it gets colder
misconceptions: days get longer going into autumn
it gets warmer going into autumn

question: When neither hemisphere is tilted towards or away from the Sun (spring and autumn), what is true about the length of day and night?
answer: Day and night are roughly equal in length for both hemispheres
accept: days and nights are about the same
daylight and darkness are roughly equal
misconceptions: one hemisphere still has much longer days
day and night are equal only at the poles

question: Explain the full chain of reasoning for why the southern hemisphere has winter when its pole is tilted away from the Sun.
answer: When the South Pole is tilted away from the Sun, sunlight hits the southern hemisphere at a glancing angle
glancing light is spread over a larger area, so it is less concentrated and gives less warming
therefore the southern hemisphere is colder, which is winter
accept: glancing light is weaker per area so it is cooler
spread-out light means less warming, giving winter
misconceptions: winter is because the hemisphere is further from the Sun
the Sun gives out less heat in winter

question: What is weight?
answer: The force of gravity acting on a mass
accept: the pull of gravity on an object
how much a planet pulls you down
misconceptions: how much matter is in an object
the amount of stuff in an object

question: Write the word equation used to calculate weight.
answer: weight = mass × gravitational field strength
accept: W = m × g
weight = mass times gravitational field strength

question: Explain why an astronaut's mass stays the same when they travel to the Moon, but their weight changes.
answer: Mass is the amount of matter in the astronaut, and this does not change
Weight is the force of gravity, and the Moon's gravity is weaker than Earth's so the weight is different
accept: the amount of matter is unchanged
the Moon's gravitational field strength is smaller
misconceptions: mass and weight are the same so both change
the astronaut loses matter in space
there is no gravity at all on the Moon

question: What causes an object to have weight?
answer: The gravitational field/pull of a planet (or moon/star) acting on the object
This pull acts towards the centre of the planet
accept: the planet's gravity pulls on it
the gravitational pull of whatever it is on or near
misconceptions: the amount of matter in the object

question: Is weight a contact or a non-contact force?
answer: Non-contact force
accept: non-contact

question: In which direction does the weight of an object always act?
answer: Towards the centre of the planet
accept: towards the centre of the Earth
downwards towards the planet's centre
misconceptions: straight down relative to the room

question: Where would a person be weightless?
answer: In deep space, far from any planet/moon/star
accept: out in deep space away from gravitational fields
where no planet pulls on them
misconceptions: at the top of a tall mountain

question: What is meant by gravitational field strength?
answer: How strong a planet's pull of gravity is (the force per kilogram of mass)
accept: how big the pull of gravity is
the number of newtons of force for each kilogram
misconceptions: the total weight of a planet

question: A planet has a larger mass than Earth. Explain what this means for the gravitational field strength and the weight of an object placed on it.
answer: A larger mass gives a stronger gravitational field / bigger gravitational field strength
So the same object would have a greater weight on that planet
accept: more mass means a bigger pull of gravity
the object is pulled down with more force
misconceptions: the weight would stay the same
a bigger planet means weaker gravity

question: Explain why an astronaut can jump higher on the Moon than on Earth, even though they are the same person.
answer: The Moon's gravitational field is weaker than Earth's, so the astronaut's weight is less
There is less force pulling them down, so they can jump higher (their mass is unchanged)
accept: the Moon pulls down with less force
weaker gravity on the Moon means smaller weight
misconceptions: the astronaut has less mass on the Moon
there is no gravity on the Moon so they float
they become lighter because they lose matter

question: State two ways the weight of an object on Earth could be decreased.
answer: Decrease the object's mass
Take the object to a place with a weaker gravitational field (e.g. a smaller planet or moon)
accept: make it have less matter
move it somewhere with smaller gravitational field strength
misconceptions: move it higher off the ground

question: A student says: 'My mass and weight are the same and both stay the same out in space.' Correct this statement.
answer: Mass is the amount of matter and never changes, so it stays the same in space
Weight is the gravitational force and changes (it becomes zero in deep space), so mass and weight are not the same
accept: mass stays the same but weight changes
weight is a force, mass is not
misconceptions: mass and weight are the same thing
weight stays the same but mass changes

question: A box of cereal is labelled '500 g' on the side. Is this telling us the mass or the weight of the cereal?
answer: The mass
accept: mass

question: What is the name of the force that pulls us down when we jump?
answer: Weight
accept: the force of weight

question: Explain why a person standing on the opposite side of the Earth to you does not fall off into space.
answer: Weight always acts towards the centre of the planet
So everyone on Earth is pulled towards the centre, holding them onto the surface wherever they stand
accept: gravity pulls everyone towards the Earth's centre
'down' means towards the centre of the Earth
misconceptions: down is the same direction everywhere in space
they are held on by air pressure
gravity only pulls things on the top of the Earth

question: Give two differences between mass and weight.
answer: Mass is the amount of matter; weight is the force of gravity
Mass is measured in kilograms; weight is measured in newtons (mass stays the same everywhere, weight changes between planets)
accept: weight is a force, mass is not
mass is in kg and weight is in N
mass is constant but weight depends on the planet

question: Describe what happens to the weight of an object as its mass increases, while it stays on the same planet.
answer: The weight increases as the mass increases
accept: it gets heavier / more weight
weight goes up
misconceptions: only the mass changes, not the weight

question: In everyday life people use the words 'mass' and 'weight' as if they mean the same thing. Suggest why this confusion happens.
answer: We spend all our time on Earth, where the gravitational field strength is the same everywhere
So the same mass always has the same weight, and we never notice them being different
accept: on Earth gravity is the same so mass and weight always go together
we never travel to other planets to see weight change
misconceptions: mass and weight really are the same thing
weight does not depend on gravity
mass is also measured in newtons

question: Calculate the weight of a 2 kg object on Earth, where g = 10 N/kg.
accept: 20 newtons
working: weight = mass × g = 2 × 10 = 20 N

question: Calculate the weight of a 6 kg object on Earth, where g = 10 N/kg.
accept: 60 newtons
working: weight = mass × g = 6 × 10 = 60 N

question: Calculate the weight of a 0.5 kg object on Earth, where g = 10 N/kg.
answer: 5 N
accept: 5 newtons
working: weight = mass × g = 0.5 × 10 = 5 N

question: Calculate the weight of a 4 kg object on Jupiter, where g = 26 N/kg.
accept: 104 newtons
working: weight = mass × g = 4 × 26 = 104 N

question: Calculate the weight of a 4 kg object on the Moon, where g = 1.6 N/kg.
accept: 6.4 newtons
working: weight = mass × g = 4 × 1.6 = 6.4 N

question: Calculate the weight of a 4 kg object on Earth, where g = 10 N/kg.
accept: 40 newtons
working: weight = mass × g = 4 × 10 = 40 N

question: Calculate the weight of a 200 kg object on Earth, where g = 10 N/kg.
accept: 2000 newtons
working: weight = mass × g = 200 × 10 = 2000 N

question: Calculate the weight of a 0.1 kg object on Jupiter, where g = 26 N/kg.
accept: 2.6 newtons
working: weight = mass × g = 0.1 × 26 = 2.6 N

question: An object weighs 100 N on Earth, where g = 10 N/kg. Calculate its mass.
answer: 10 kg
accept: 10 kilograms
working: mass = weight ÷ g = 100 ÷ 10 = 10 kg

question: Calculate the weight of a 10 kg object on the Moon, where g = 1.6 N/kg.
accept: 16 newtons
working: weight = mass × g = 10 × 1.6 = 16 N

question: Which of these is the correct unit for weight?
answer: Newtons (N)
distractors: kilograms are the unit of mass, not weight
grams are also a unit of mass, often confused with weight
N/kg is the unit of gravitational field strength, not weight

question: Which statement best describes mass?
answer: A measure of how much matter is in an object
options: A measure of how much matter is in an object
The force of gravity acting on an object
The pull of a planet measured in newtons
How strong a planet's gravity is
distractors: this describes weight, which is a force
this also describes weight, measured in newtons
this describes gravitational field strength
misconceptions: mass is the force of gravity
mass is the same as gravitational field strength

question: A person travels from Earth to the Moon. Which of the following changes?
answer: Their weight
options: The amount of matter in them
The number of atoms in them
distractors: mass is the amount of matter and does not change between planets
the amount of matter is the mass, which stays the same
the number of atoms is fixed, so this does not change

question: On which of these would a 1 kg object have the greatest weight?
answer: Jupiter (g = 26 N/kg)
distractors: Earth's field is strong but smaller than Jupiter's
the Moon's field is much weaker than Earth's or Jupiter's
in deep space there is no gravitational field, so weight is zero
misconceptions: weight is greatest on Earth because that is where we live
the Moon gives the most weight

question: Why does an object have more weight on Earth than on the Moon?
answer: Earth has a stronger gravitational field than the Moon
options: Earth has a stronger gravitational field than the Moon
The object gains mass on Earth
The Moon has no gravity at all
The object has more atoms on Earth
distractors: mass does not change between Earth and the Moon
the Moon does have gravity, just a weaker pull (1.6 N/kg)
the number of atoms (mass) is the same in both places
misconceptions: the object gains mass on Earth

question: A bag of potatoes is labelled '1 kg'. What does this value tell us?
answer: Its mass
options: The force of gravity on it
distractors: weight is a force measured in newtons, not kilograms
the force of gravity is the weight, measured in newtons
gravitational field strength is measured in N/kg, not kg
misconceptions: the label gives the gravitational force
the label gives the field strength

question: An astronaut floats freely, completely weightless. Where are they most likely to be?
answer: In deep space, far from any planet
options: In deep space, far from any planet
At the top of a tall mountain on Earth
Floating in the sea on Earth
distractors: the Moon still has a gravitational field, so they would have weight
gravity still acts at the top of a mountain, so they have weight
in the sea they are still pulled down by Earth's gravity
misconceptions: astronauts are weightless on the Moon
being high up makes you weightless
floating in water means zero weight
